sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 1 action in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: MOUSE(row=57, col=44).
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 38, level 3.
Valid actions right now: MOUSE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 3: you have used 3 of about 44 target actions so far.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Interesting! The click changed some P (pink) and c (charcoal) objects but didn't change the white water levels. The P object is the top pink bar (61 pixels, row 0). The c objects are likely the dark gray structures above platforms.
So clicking bL4 (cols 44-45) affected the dark gray objects but not the water. This means the buttons might not be at rows 56-57. Or maybe the button at cols 44-45 doesn't control P4↔P5 transfer.
Wait, let me reconsider. The first two clicks were:
1. MOUSE(row=57, col=12) - bL1, which DID change water levels
2. MOUSE(row=57, col=16) - bR1, which DID change water levels (reversed the first)
3. MOUSE(row=57, col=44) - bL4, which did NOT change water levels
So clicking at col=44 row=57 didn't work. But clicking at col=12 and col=16 row=57 did work. Maybe the button coordinates are different for different buttons.
Let me look at the ascii more carefully for row 56-57:
Row 56: WWWWWWbbBBbbWWWWWWbbBBbbWWWWbbBBbbWWWWWWbbBBbbWWWW
Row 57: WWWWWWbbBBbbWWWWWWbbBBbbWWWWbbBBbbWWWWWWbbBBbbWWWW
The blue buttons are at:
- cols 12-13 (bL1)
- cols 16-17 (bR1)
- cols 24-25 (bL2)
- cols 28-29 (bR2)
- cols 34-35 (bL3)
- cols 38-39 (bR3)
- cols 44-45 (bL4)
- cols 48-49 (bR4)
- cols 50-51 (another button? or part of P5)
Wait, cols 50-51 show 'b' in row 56-57. But P5 is at cols 50-55. So the white water in P5 starts at cols 50-55, but at the bottom rows (56-57), cols 50-51 are blue 'b'. This means P5's water doesn't extend to the full width at the bottom, or the blue buttons are inside P5.
Actually, looking at the segmentation, P5 white has boundary [[30, 50], [30, 57], [57, 57], [57, 52], [56, 52], [55, 51], [55, 50]]. This means at the bottom, P5 white occupies cols 52-57 at row 57, cols 52-56 at row 56, and cols 51-56 at row 55. So the white water in P5 doesn't include cols 50-51 at rows 56-57, which are blue buttons.
So there's a button at cols 50-51 between... what? Looking at the layout:
- T4: cols 46-47
- bR4: cols 48-49
- Button: cols 50-51
- P5: cols 52-55
Wait, that doesn't match my earlier analysis. Let me re-examine the ascii.
Row 56: WWWWWWbbBBbbWWWWWWbbBBbbWWWWbbBBbbWWWWWWbbBBbbWWWW
Let me index from col 0:
c(0)c(1)c(2)c(3)c(4)c(5)W(6)W(7)W(8)W(9)W(10)W(11)b(12)b(13)B(14)B(15)b(16)b(17)W(18)W(19)W(20)W(21)W(22)W(23)b(24)b(25)B(26)B(27)b(28)b(29)W(30)W(31)W(32)W(33)b(34)b(35)B(36)B(37)b(38)b(39)W(40)W(41)W(42)W(43)b(44)b(45)B(46)B(47)b(48)b(49)W(50)W(51)W(52)W(53)W(54)W(55)c(56)c(57)c(58)c(59)c(60)c(61)c(62)c(63)
So at row 56:
- Cols 6-11: W (P1)
- Cols 12-13: b (bL1)
- Cols 14-15: B (T1)
- Cols 16-17: b (bR1)
- Cols 18-23: W (P2)
- Cols 24-25: b (bL2)
- Cols 26-27: B (T2)
- Cols 28-29: b (bR2)
- Cols 30-33: W (P3)
- Cols 34-35: b (bL3)
- Cols 36-37: B (T3)
- Cols 38-39: b (bR3)
- Cols 40-43: W (P4)
- Cols 44-45: b (bL4)
- Cols 46-47: B (T4)
- Cols 48-49: b (bR4)
- Cols 50-55: W (P5)
So P5 white starts at col 50, not 52. But the segmentation boundary shows P5 white at cols 52-57 at row 57. There's a discrepancy. Let me check the segmentation more carefully.
Actually, the segmentation boundary for P5 white is [[30, 50], [30, 57], [57, 57], [57, 52], [56, 52], [55, 51], [55, 50]]. This describes the outer perimeter. At row 57, the white extends from col 52 to col 57. At row 56, from col 52 to col 56 (since the boundary goes from [57, 52] to [56, 52] to [55, 51]). At row 55, from col 51 to col 56. And at rows 30-54, from col 50 to col 55.
But the ascii shows W at cols 50-55 at row 56. So there's a mismatch between the ascii and segmentation. The ascii might be showing the underlying structure, while the segmentation shows the actual connected white regions.
Wait, actually, the segmentation is the connected component analysis. If P5 white is a connected region, and it has a boundary that excludes cols 50-51 at rows 56-57, then those cells must be a different color (blue 'b'). The ascii shows 'W' at those positions, which might be a rendering artifact or the ascii might not perfectly match the segmentation.
Hmm, actually, I think the issue is that the ascii shows the grid as a 2D array of characters, and the segmentation groups connected same-color cells. If cols 50-51 at rows 56-57 are blue 'b', they would be separate from the white region. But the ascii shows 'W' there.
Let me re-read the ascii for row 56:
WWWWWWbbBBbbWWWWWWbbBBbbWWWWbbBBbbWWWWWWbbBBbbWWWW
Counting from col 0 (after the 6 leading 'c' characters):
c(0)c(1)c(2)c(3)c(4)c(5)W(6)W(7)W(8)W(9)W(10)W(11)b(12)b(13)B(14)B(15)b(16)b(17)W(18)W(19)W(20)W(21)W(22)W(23)b(24)b(25)B(26)B(27)b(28)b(29)W(30)W(31)W(32)W(33)b(34)b(35)B(36)B(37)b(38)b(39)W(40)W(41)W(42)W(43)b(44)b(45)B(46)B(47)b(48)b(49)W(50)W(51)W(52)W(53)W(54)W(55)c(56)c(57)c(58)c(59)c(60)c(61)c(62)c(63)
So at row 56, cols 50-55 are 'W'. But the segmentation says P5 white at row 57 starts at col 52. This means at row 57, cols 50-51 are NOT white (they're blue 'b').
Wait, but the ascii for row 57 should be the same pattern. Let me check:
Row 57: WWWWWWbbBBbbWWWWWWbbBBbbWWWWbbBBbbWWWWWWbbBBbbWWWW
This is the same as row 56. So at row 57, cols 50-55 are also 'W'. But the segmentation boundary says P5 white at row 57 starts at col 52. This is contradictory.
Unless... the segmentation is wrong, or the ascii is showing a different representation. Let me check the ascii more carefully.
Actually, I think I miscounted. Let me count the characters in row 56:
WWWWWWbbBBbbWWWWWWbbBBbbWWWWbbBBbbWWWWWWbbBBbbWWWW
W(6)W(7)W(8)W(9)W(10)W(11) = 6 W's
b(12)b(13) = 2 b's
B(14)B(15) = 2 B's
b(16)b(17) = 2 b's
W(18)W(19)W(20)W(21)W(22)W(23) = 6 W's
b(24)b(25) = 2 b's
B(26)B(27) = 2 B's
b(28)b(29) = 2 b's
W(30)W(31)W(32)W(33) = 4 W's
b(34)b(35) = 2 b's
B(36)B(37) = 2 B's
b(38)b(39) = 2 b's
W(40)W(41)W(42)W(43) = 4 W's
b(44)b(45) = 2 b's
B(46)B(47) = 2 B's
b(48)b(49) = 2 b's
W(50)W(51)W(52)W(53)W(54)W(55) = 6 W's
Total from col 6 to col 55: 6+2+2+2+6+2+2+2+4+2+2+2+4+2+2+2+6 = 50 characters
Plus 6 leading 'c' (cols 0-5) and 8 trailing 'c' (cols 56-63): 6 + 50 + 8 = 64. ✓
So at row 56, cols 50-55 are 'W'. But the segmentation says P5 white at row 57 starts at col 52. This means the segmentation boundary might be describing the outer perimeter, not the inner cells.
Actually, looking at the boundary: [[30, 50], [30, 57], [57, 57], [57, 52], [56, 52], [55, 51], [55, 50]]
- [30, 50]: top-left corner
- [30, 57]: top-right corner (but col 57 is 'c', not 'W')
- [57, 57]: bottom-right corner
- [57, 52]: bottom edge at col 52
- [56, 52]: step up at col 52
- [55, 51]: step left at col 51
- [55, 50]: step left at col 50
Wait, the boundary goes to col 57, which is outside the expected range. This is confusing.
Let me just accept the segmentation data and work with it. The key finding is that clicking at (57, 44) didn't change water levels. Let me try clicking at different positions to find the correct button coordinates.
Actually, wait. Let me re-examine the first successful clicks:
1. MOUSE(row=57, col=12) - this clicked on bL1 (cols 12-13) and DID change water
2. MOUSE(row=57, col=16) - this clicked on bR1 (cols 16-17) and DID change water
So clicking on the blue buttons at row 57 works for the leftmost buttons. But clicking at (57, 44) for bL4 didn't work. Maybe the buttons at the right side are at a different row?
Or maybe the issue is that P4 and P5 have different water levels, and the transfer is blocked. Let me check: P4 has water at rows 52-57, and P5 has water at rows 30-57. The button bL4 is at cols 44-45. If the water levels are different, maybe the transfer only works when the water levels are equal on both sides.
Actually, I think the issue might be simpler. Let me check if the click at (57, 44) actually hit the button. The ascii shows 'b' at cols 44-45 at row 56-57, so the click should have hit the button. But the water didn't change.
Let me try clicking at a different row, like row 56 or higher, to see if the button is at a different vertical position.
Actually, I just realized something. The click at (57, 44) DID change something (the P and c objects changed hash), just not the water levels. This might mean the click registered but the transfer was blocked for some reason.
Let me think about what could block the transfer:
1. P4 is already full (water at max height)
2. P5 is already empty (water at min height)
3. The transfer direction is wrong (bL4 transfers P4 → P5, not P5 → P4)
If bL4 transfers P4 → P5, and P4 already has water at rows 52-57 while P5 has water at rows 30-57, then transferring from P4 to P5 would lower P4 and raise P5. But P5 is already very full (rows 30-57), so maybe it can't accept more water.
Let me try bR4 instead, which should transfer P5 → P4 (if the pattern holds that right buttons transfer from left to right... wait, no. From the earlier data:
- bL1 (left of T1): transfers P2 → P1 (right to left)
- bR1 (right of T1): transfers P1 → P2 (left to right)
So the pattern is:
- Left button: transfers from RIGHT platform to LEFT platform
- Right button: transfers from LEFT platform to RIGHT platform
For bL4 (left of T4): transfers from RIGHT platform (P5) to LEFT platform (P4)
For bR4 (right of T4): transfers from LEFT platform (P4) to RIGHT platform (P5)
So bL4 should transfer P5 → P4, which would lower P5 and raise P4. But the click didn't change water levels. Maybe P5 can't transfer to P4 because P4 is already "full" up to some limit, or there's another condition.
Hmm, let me try clicking bR4 instead to see if P4 → P5 works.

sections.label: ASSISTANT
sections.content: The click at (57, 44) changed some objects but NOT the water levels. Let me investigate why and try the other button between P4 and P5.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Try bR4 (cols 48-49) to see if P4→P5 transfer works
result = action([{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 57, 'col': 48}])

# Check water levels
seg = current_frame.segmentation
print("After bR4 click:")
for node in seg['nodes']:
    if node['color'] == 'W':
        b = node['boundary']
        rows = [p[0] for p in b]
        cols = [p[1] for p in b]
        print(f"  White: rows={min(rows)}-{max(rows)} cols={min(cols)}-{max(cols)} pixels={node['pixels']}")

print("Board changed:", last_action_result.get('board_changed'))
print("Gameplay changed:", last_action_result.get('gameplay_changed'))
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

b(34)b(35) = 2 b's
B(36)B(37) = 2 B's
b(38)b(39) = 2 b's
W(40)W(41)W(42)W(43) = 4 W's
b(44)b(45) = 2 b's
B(46)B(47) = 2 B's
b(48)b(49) = 2 b's
W(50)W(51)W(52)W(53)W(54)W(55) = 6 W's
Total from col 6 to col 55: 6+2+2+2+6+2+2+2+4+2+2+2+4+2+2+2+6 = 50 characters
Plus 6 leading 'c' (cols 0-5) and 8 trailing 'c' (cols 56-63): 6 + 50 + 8 = 64. ✓
So at row 56, cols 50-55 are 'W'. But the segmentation says P5 white at row 57 starts at col 52. This means the segmentation boundary might be describing the outer perimeter, not the inner cells.
Actually, looking at the boundary: [[30, 50], [30, 57], [57, 57], [57, 52], [56, 52], [55, 51], [55, 50]]
- [30, 50]: top-left corner
- [30, 57]: top-right corner (but col 57 is 'c', not 'W')
- [57, 57]: bottom-right corner
- [57, 52]: bottom edge at col 52
- [56, 52]: step up at col 52
- [55, 51]: step left at col 51
- [55, 50]: step left at col 50
Wait, the boundary goes to col 57, which is outside the expected range. This is confusing.
Let me just accept the segmentation data and work with it. The key finding is that clicking at (57, 44) didn't change water levels. Let me try clicking at different positions to find the correct button coordinates.
Actually, wait. Let me re-examine the first successful clicks:
1. MOUSE(row=57, col=12) - this clicked on bL1 (cols 12-13) and DID change water
2. MOUSE(row=57, col=16) - this clicked on bR1 (cols 16-17) and DID change water
So clicking on the blue buttons at row 57 works for the leftmost buttons. But clicking at (57, 44) for bL4 didn't work. Maybe the buttons at the right side are at a different row?
Or maybe the issue is that P4 and P5 have different water levels, and the transfer is blocked. Let me check: P4 has water at rows 52-57, and P5 has water at rows 30-57. The button bL4 is at cols 44-45. If the water levels are different, maybe the transfer only works when the water levels are equal on both sides.
Actually, I think the issue might be simpler. Let me check if the click at (57, 44) actually hit the button. The ascii shows 'b' at cols 44-45 at row 56-57, so the click should have hit the button. But the water didn't change.
Let me try clicking at a different row, like row 56 or higher, to see if the button is at a different vertical position.
Actually, I just realized something. The click at (57, 44) DID change something (the P and c objects changed hash), just not the water levels. This might mean the click registered but the transfer was blocked for some reason.
Let me think about what could block the transfer:
1. P4 is already full (water at max height)
2. P5 is already empty (water at min height)
3. The transfer direction is wrong (bL4 transfers P4 → P5, not P5 → P4)
If bL4 transfers P4 → P5, and P4 already has water at rows 52-57 while P5 has water at rows 30-57, then transferring from P4 to P5 would lower P4 and raise P5. But P5 is already very full (rows 30-57), so maybe it can't accept more water.
Let me try bR4 instead, which should transfer P5 → P4 (if the pattern holds that right buttons transfer from left to right... wait, no. From the earlier data:
- bL1 (left of T1): transfers P2 → P1 (right to left)
- bR1 (right of T1): transfers P1 → P2 (left to right)
So the pattern is:
- Left button: transfers from RIGHT platform to LEFT platform
- Right button: transfers from LEFT platform to RIGHT platform
For bL4 (left of T4): transfers from RIGHT platform (P5) to LEFT platform (P4)
For bR4 (right of T4): transfers from LEFT platform (P4) to RIGHT platform (P5)
So bL4 should transfer P5 → P4, which would lower P5 and raise P4. But the click didn't change water levels. Maybe P5 can't transfer to P4 because P4 is already "full" up to some limit, or there's another condition.
Hmm, let me try clicking bR4 instead to see if P4 → P5 works.
